query: recent evidence on spontaneous resolution of ventricular arrhythmias in athletes

retrieved_docs.title: Atrioventricular Reciprocating Tachycardia -- Pearls and Other Issues -- SVT in Sports
retrieved_docs.content: Athletes with frequent SVTs should be evaluated for an underlying cardiac or thyroid disease and the use of stimulants or performance-enhancing drugs. If SVT, such as AVRT, occurs during exercise with very rapid heart rates, it may cause hemodynamic impairment; thus, radiofrequency ablation is recommended as a treatment option. For athletes with sporadic SVT that is not associated with exercise and is well-tolerated, the sport does not pose a high risk of loss of consciousness during the SVT (in contrast to activities such as diving, piloting, and horse riding). In cases where radiofrequency ablation has been unsuccessful, these athletes may still be eligible to compete. Certain antiarrhythmic drugs are discouraged in athletes. Sodium channel blockers have a limited ability to prevent SVT recurrence during exercise. Beta-blockers reduce athletic performance and are banned by the World Anti-Doping Agency in certain sports requiring precision and stillness, such as golf, shooting, and archery. [19] [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Atrioventricular Reciprocating Tachycardia -- Pearls and Other Issues -- WPW syndrome in Athletes and Risk for AVRT and Sudden Cardiac Death
retrieved_docs.content: Although many young athletes with WPW syndrome remain asymptomatic, AVRT remains a possibility. Managing ventricular preexcitation in affected athletes is challenging due to the small but ever-present risk of sudden cardiac death, which appears to be higher in individuals younger than 40, especially males. [4] The current guidelines recommend performing an electrophysiologic study to risk-stratify the accessory pathway, irrespective of symptoms, especially in competitive athletes (and individuals with high-risk occupations such as airline pilots). Radiofrequency ablation or cryoablation is recommended if the accessory pathway is considered as high risk based on factors such as a shortest preexcited R-R interval or SPERRI of ≤250 msec, an effective refractory period or ERP of ≤250 msec, the presence of multiple accessory pathways, and the presence of an inducible accessory pathway-mediated tachycardia. Asymptomatic individuals stratified as having a low-risk accessory pathway can be allowed to participate in competitive sports, as AVRT over a concealed accessory pathway is not listed as a cause of sudden cardiac death during exercise in those with normal cardiac anatomy and function. [19] It appears that anteroseptal accessory pathways are more likely to be linked with the development of atrial fibrillation. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Sudden Death in Athletes -- Evaluation -- Normal Physiologic Findings On ECGs
retrieved_docs.content: Athletes may have findings on their ECG that would otherwise be considered abnormal in the general population. Sinus bradycardia (under 60 beats per minute) is among the most common benign abnormal ECG findings, but very low heart rates should not be ignored. Infrequent PVCs and premature atrial contractions (PACs) are also considered normal among the general population and physically active people. However, specific findings on ECGs considered normal among athletes but abnormal in other populations include: Left ventricular hypertrophy (QRS voltage criteria) Right ventricular hypertrophy (QRS voltage criteria) Sinus arrhythmia Early repolarisation First-degree atrioventricular block

retrieved_docs.title: Sudden Death in Athletes -- Prognosis -- Catecholaminergic Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia
retrieved_docs.content: Other conditions may either have an unknown prognosis or have a highly variable prognosis dependent on response to treatment. Lastly, compliance with treatment is also a concern, especially in younger athletes. Many athletes may need to abstain from sports for some time until they receive a thorough evaluation and a treatment regime is finalized. Compliance with the period of abstinence, adherence to the treatment regime, and avoidance of other aggravating factors are all instrumental in the prognosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Sports Participation Evaluation -- Clinical Significance -- Cardiac Conditions Associated With Cardiac Arrest in Athletes
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertrophic Obstructive Cardiomyopathy (the most common cause of sudden death in young athletes) [10] [11] [12] Coronary Artery Anomaly (the second most common cause of sudden death in young athletes) Coronary Artery Disease (the most common overall cause of death in athletes of any age due to its relatively high prevalence among older athletes) [13] Arrhythmias such as ventricular tachycardia are among the most frequent causes of sudden death. [14] [13] Anabolic Steroid Induced Cardiac Disease, including left ventricular hypertrophy [15] Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Dysplasia Dilated Cardiomyopathy Left Ventricular Noncompaction Congenital Long QT Syndrome Short QT Syndrome Brugada Syndrome Wolff Parkinson White Syndrome Catecholaminergic Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia Early Repolarization Syndrome Myocarditis Commotio Cordis associated with sudden chest wall impact, usually in contact sports

retrieved_docs.title: Sudden Death in Athletes -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Explain the onset of the cardiac problems that may precede sudden cardiac death. Describe the sports clearance physical exam and what findings might prompt the consideration of a condition putting the individual at risk for sudden cardiac death. Describe the management of the various cardiac etiologies that lead to sudden death in athletes. Examine interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to prevent missed opportunities to diagnose and treat patients at risk for sudden cardiac death. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Sports Participation Evaluation -- Function -- Further Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiogram (ECG): The decision to undergo testing should be discussed between the physician and the patient. It is critical to distinguish between abnormal ECG findings within the general population and expected results in athletes. Concerning findings in athletes include accessory pathways, short PR intervals, long or short QT intervals, epsilon waves, T-wave inversions, and excessive premature ventricular contractions. Normal ECG findings in athletes include bradycardia, sinus arrhythmia, ventricular enlargement, and first-degree atrioventricular block. Distinguishing benign and pathologic findings can reduce unnecessary diagnostic testing and treatments. [8] Consulting cardiologists often perform echocardiography when clinically significant conditions are diagnosed.

retrieved_docs.title: Sudden Death in Athletes -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The function of the heart is to effectively pump blood throughout the body, and hence disturbing the blood flow and/or the rhythm of the heartbeats negatively impacts the heart's functional goals. Structural defects prevent the heart from effectively pumping blood, especially during times of increased demand. Ion channel defects disturb the regularity of necessary electrolytes, which maintain the heart's rate and rhythm. Consequently, untreated, structural defects can result in arrhythmia, while persistent arrhythmia may lead to structural heart changes. An example of this is dilated cardiomyopathy which can develop due to an excessive premature ventricular contraction (PVC) burden.

retrieved_docs.title: Reentrant Arrhythmias -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of reentrant ventricular tachycardia is made on a 12-lead ECG. A 12-lead ECG in sinus rhythm should be compared with ECG during tachycardia. A 12-lead ECG sinus rhythm should be evaluated for myocardial ischemia or infarction. For patients with exercise-related symptoms, an exercise ECG is recommended to document exercise-induced ventricular arrhythmias. For symptomatic patients with undocumented ventricular arrhythmia, an ambulatory ECG is recommended to assess the correlation of symptoms with arrhythmia. While those patients with infrequent symptoms, implantable cardiac monitors may help in the diagnosis. [56] All patients presenting with ventricular arrhythmias should have an echocardiogram to assess cardiac structure and function, while cardiac magnetic resonance imaging is recommended in a selected group of patients to diagnose the underlying cause of ventricular arrhythmias. Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging also provides information about the prognosis of patients with reentrant ventricular tachycardia. [57] An invasive electrophysiology study is no longer recommended to diagnose reentrant ventricular arrhythmias; however, it can be used for risk stratification of patients who do not meet the criteria of primary prevention implantable cardioverter defibrillator. [58]

retrieved_docs.title: Sudden Death in Athletes -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Heart disease in athletes involves a combination of general treatments and specific treatments. From a lifestyle standpoint, many athletes may be asked to avoid strenuous exercise and other aggravating factors for a brief period. Physical and/or mental stress increases cardiac demand but also may trigger potentially fatal arrhythmias. Upon establishing a long-term treatment regime, there should be a process of shared decision-making in regards to the athlete returning to their sport of choice. The athlete will undergo a phase of risk stratification, and many variables will play a role in determining their risk of sudden death and the appropriate treatment regime. Aside from the athlete's physiology, their specific sport also can significantly affect the long-term risk. Although earlier views in literature were that many forms of heart disease in athletes should end their athletic careers, the literature in 2019 holds the view that many athletes can safely return to their sport of choice if they undergo a correct treatment process. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Reentrant Arrhythmias -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Outline the stepwise approach for the evaluation of reentrant arrhythmias. Summarize treatment modalities available for a patient with a reentrant arrhythmia. Review the pathophysiology of various reentrant arrhythmias. Describe how the coordination of an interprofessional team can lead to a more rapid diagnosis of reentrant arrhythmias and subsequently decrease associated morbidity and mortality in affected patients. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Sudden Death in Athletes -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Medical management generally involves the goal of increasing blood flow and lowering the risk of arrhythmias. Hence, beta-blockers are a commonly used medication in these patients. Calcium channel blockers may also be an option. Surgical and percutaneous choices also exist for certain structural defects, such as surgical septal myomectomy or alcohol septal ablation in HOCM. In congenital arrhythmias, ablation may be performed to eradicate the electrical source of the arrhythmia. When the risk of sudden death is appreciated, an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) may be placed in case the patient goes into cardiac arrest. ICD placement has dramatically improved the prognosis in all athletes with congenital heart defects and has even allowed for continued sports participation. The last option in certain defects is a heart transplant. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Sudden Death in Athletes -- Prognosis -- Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Dysplasia
retrieved_docs.content: Brugada syndrome: Risk stratification is done to assess the need for interventional treatment. Asymptomatic patients have a near-normal life expectancy, whereas symptomatic patients can have a life expectancy as low as 40 years old. No strong and direct association to athletics. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Electrophysiologic Testing -- Indications -- Indications of the EPS
retrieved_docs.content: Supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) The SVT or narrow complex tachycardia is frequent and associated with a heart rate above 100/minute and a QRS complex shorter than 120ms. Narrow QRS complex SVT  Narrow QRS tachycardia that usually needs EPS: regular narrow QRS tachycardias - sinus tachycardia, inappropriate sinus tachycardia, sinus nodal reentrant tachycardia, focal atrial tachycardia, atrial flutter with stable atrioventricular conduction, atrioventricular nodal reentrant tachycardia, junctional ectopic tachycardia, orthodromic atrioventricular reentrant tachycardia and irregular narrow QRS tachycardias- atrial fibrillation, focal atrial tachycardia with variable atrioventricular block, atrial flutter with variable atrioventricular block, multifocal atrial tachycardia. EPS should be performed to diagnose SVT, particularly when anticipating catheter ablation. Because of the narrow QRS complex, a high septal ventricular tachycardia can mimic an SVT. Accessory pathways- the majority of accessory pathways conduct both anterogradely and retrogradely. The 2019 ESC Guidelines (the first guideline update in 16 years) regarding the management of patients with SVT recommends (class I) EPS in asymptomatic patients (including athletes and patients with high occupational risk) for assessment of sudden cardiac death risk. Catheter ablation is indicated when EPS with the use of isoprenaline identifies high-risk features: anterograde effective refractory period below or equal to 250ms, RR minimal pre-excited interval during atrial fibrillation less than or equal to 250 ms, multiple accessory pathways, induction of an accessory pathway-dependent tachycardia. [5] Wide QRS complex SVT - regular (SVT with an QRS complex over 120 ms): antidromic atrioventricular reentrant tachycardia, all supraventricular tachycardias with aberration or preexisting or rate-dependent bundle branch block, atrial tachycardia with preexcitation having the accessory pathway as a bystander and irregular: atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter or tachycardia with variable atrioventricular block, conducted with aberration, antidromic atrioventricular tachycardia due to a nodo-ventricular or nodo-fascicular accessory pathway with variable ventriculoatrial conduction. The EPS in those patients aims to establish the underlying mechanism and to guide catheter ablation.

retrieved_docs.content: Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome Congenital long QT syndrome Acquired long QT syndrome Brugada syndrome Atrioventricular blocks Catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia Short QT syndrome Early repolarisation syndrome Idiopathic ventricular fibrillation Mixed sodium channel disease Stray electric currents created by the athlete's chest muscles Environmentally created static electricity

retrieved_docs.title: Sudden Death in Athletes -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Athletes should be questioned about the usage of performance-enhancing drugs (PEDs), specifically anabolic steroids and stimulants. These agents induce the risk of sudden cardiac death through multiple mechanisms. The most common mechanism is left ventricular hypertrophy in anabolic steroid users, while arrhythmias and vascular injury also occur. High doses of testosterone, use of growth factors, classic agents such as stanozolol, and commonly used injectable drugs like trenbolone; all induce severe left ventricular hypertrophy. Different athletes experience this to variable degrees, and hence knowledge of this history is critical. Also, the usage of stimulants increases the likelihood of arrhythmias, especially in those with existing cardiac illnesses. Some pre-workout supplements contain potentially dangerous compounds that induce a high burden of ectopic heartbeats, prolong QT, and can produce clinical symptoms. Examples of such compounds include 1,3-dimethylamylamine (DMAA), which has been banned in numerous countries. Lastly, blood doping and erythropoietin (EPO) use is of concern, particularly in endurance athletes, and has correlated with sudden cardiac arrest. [16] [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.content: induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, and treatment of ventricular tachycardia target the intrinsic accelerated ventricular rhythm (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل) and its subtype ventricular acceleration (التسارع البطيني). Understanding these interconnected entities—electrical conduction abnormalities, structural cardiac changes, specific arrhythmic syndromes, diagnostic measurements, and therapeutic interventions—is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and management of patients with cardiac rhythm disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 924
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac electrophysiology study (دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية) is closely linked to conduction disorder (اضطراب التوصيل). Electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, and treatment of atrial fibrillation, as well as the analogous evaluations for supraventricular tachycardia and ventricular tachycardia, are each classified as types of cardiac electrophysiology study. ECG monitors for arrhythmia (ECG Monitors, Arrhythmia) and ECG recordings that show a non‑sinus rhythm (ECG: non-sinus rhythm) both have a direct relationship with, and focus on, conduction disorder. Ventricular acceleration (التسارع البطيني) is a subcategory of an intrinsic accelerated ventricular system (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل) and serves as the primary focus of the electrophysiologic evaluation and ablation treatment for ventricular tachycardia. Together, these concepts illustrate how electrophysiologic studies, ECG monitoring, and specific arrhythmic mechanisms are interrelated in the assessment and management of cardiac conduction abnormalities and ventricular acceleration disorders.

Together, these concepts illustrate how electrocardiographic monitoring and cardiac electrophysiology studies are interrelated tools for diagnosing conduction abnormalities, assessing arrhythmic risk, guiding therapeutic ablation, and supporting cardiac rehabilitation, thereby informing comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 1054
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac electrophysiology study (دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية) serves as the overarching diagnostic and therapeutic framework for evaluating and treating cardiac arrhythmias. Within this framework, electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, and treatment of atrial fibrillation is classified as a type of cardiac electrophysiology study, as is electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, and treatment of ventricular tachycardia. The ventricular‑tachycardia evaluation specifically has a focus on ventricular acceleration (التسارع البطيني), which itself is a component of an intrinsic accelerated cardiac system (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل). Both the atrial‑fibrillation and ventricular‑tachycardia procedures are closely linked to conduction disorders (اضطراب التوصيل), reflecting the central role of abnormal electrical conduction in these arrhythmias. ECG monitors that detect arrhythmia and ECG recordings showing non‑sinus rhythm also have a direct relationship with conduction disorders, underscoring the importance of surface electrocardiographic monitoring in identifying and guiding treatment of these electrophysiologic abnormalities. Together, these interrelated concepts illustrate how detailed electrophysiologic evaluation, targeted ablation, and comprehensive ECG monitoring converge to diagnose, characterize, and manage conduction disturbances and accelerated ventricular activity in patients with complex cardiac arrhythmias.

Cardiac electrophysiologic study (دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية) is defined as the invasive evaluation that includes arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic‑focus ablation, and treatment of specific tachyarrhythmias. This study is applied to atrial fibrillation, supraventricular tachycardia, and ventricular tachycardia, each being an instance of the electrophysiologic evaluation. The ventricular‑tachycardia evaluation specifically focuses on ventricular acceleration (التسارع البطيني), which itself is a subtype of an accelerated idioventricular rhythm (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل). Diagnosis relies on heart‑rate measurement, electrocardiographic monitoring, and cardiac electrophysiology studies that incorporate arrhythmia induction, electrophysiologic evaluation, and ablation of arrhythmogenic foci for conditions such as atrial fibrillation, supraventricular tachycardia, and ventricular tachycardia; the atrioventricular node is a common anatomic locus for many of these blocks and for paroxysmal tachycardia. Therapeutically, antidromic tachycardia may be treated with adenosine monophosphate (contra‑indicated in unspecified heart block), while supraventricular tachycardia and related disorders respond to the calcium‑channel blocker verapamil; meclizine hydrochloride is used to alleviate dizziness and prevent vomiting associated with conduction abnormalities. Conduction disorders are also linked to autonomic nervous system dysfunction and may coexist with congestive cardiomyopathy, which itself is clinically associated with left‑ventricular

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1141
retrieved_docs.content: The procedures described as “electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of atrial fibrillation,” “electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of supraventricular tachycardia,” and “electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of ventricular tachycardia” are all classified as a “دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية.” In the case of the ventricular‑tachycardia evaluation, the intervention specifically targets the focus “التسارع البطيني,” which is a subcategory of the broader rhythm disorder “نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل.” This hierarchy links the electrophysiologic study of ventricular tachycardia to the intrinsic accelerated cardiac rhythm represented by “نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل,” highlighting how detailed ablation strategies are organized within the overall framework of cardiac electrophysiological investigations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1138
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiographic monitoring is a broad clinical domain that includes continuous ECG observation during outpatient cardiac rehabilitation performed by physicians or other qualified health‑care professionals, as well as advanced imaging and electrophysiologic assessments such as GSPECT of the myocardium using technetium‑99m sestamibi, measurement of microvolt T‑wave alternans for ventricular arrhythmia risk stratification, and stimulation of the carotid sinus with simultaneous ECG recording. Within this domain, ECG Monitors, Arrhythmia are specifically linked to electrocardiographic monitoring and are associated with conduction disorders (اضطراب التوصيل). The entity ECG: non‑sinus rhythm is classified as a type of electrocardiographic monitoring and also has a focus on conduction disorders, underscoring its relevance to the detection and evaluation of abnormal cardiac rhythm patterns. Together, these interconnected concepts illustrate how various ECG‑based techniques and related procedures are employed to diagnose, monitor, and manage arrhythmias and conduction abnormalities, thereby supporting comprehensive patient care in cardiology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2024
retrieved_docs.content: The electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of atrial fibrillation; the electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of supraventricular tachycardia; and the electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of ventricular tachycardia are all classified as دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية, representing cardiac electrophysiology studies that deliberately induce arrhythmias, identify arrhythmogenic foci, and apply catheter ablation to treat the respective rhythm disorders. In the atrial fibrillation procedure, the primary focus is الرجفان الأذيني, whereas the ventricular tachycardia procedure targets التسارع البطيني, which is a subtype of نظمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل (self‑accelerated ventricular rhythm). Clinically, الرجفان الأذيني is associated with مرض قلبي روماتزمي, indicating a link between atrial fibrillation and rheumatic heart disease. Pharmacologically, both drospirenone and estetrol monohydrate are contraindicated in patients with الرجفان الأذيني, underscoring the need to avoid these agents in individuals with atrial fibrillation. Together, these relationships highlight the diagnostic and therapeutic role of cardiac electrophysiology studies, the specific arrhythmic targets they address, the disease associations of atrial fibrillation, and important medication contraindications that inform safe patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2557
retrieved_docs.content: The electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of atrial fibrillation and the electrophysiologic evaluation with arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, treatment of supraventricular tachycardia are both classified as a دراسة فيزيولوجية كهربائية قلبية (cardiac electrophysiological study). Each procedure involves deliberately inducing arrhythmias, identifying the arrhythmogenic focus, performing ablation of that focus, and delivering therapeutic treatment targeted to a specific rhythm disorder—atrial fibrillation in the first case and supraventricular tachycardia in the second. As forms of cardiac electrophysiological study, they provide essential diagnostic insight and therapeutic options for managing abnormal heart rhythms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2747
retrieved_docs.content: Congestive cardiomyopathy (اعتلال عضلة القلب الاحتقاني) is clinically associated with both supraventricular tachycardia (تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ) and atrial flutter (رفرفة أذينية). These arrhythmic conditions frequently arise in the setting of congestive cardiomyopathy, reflecting a close pathophysiological link in which impaired myocardial function predisposes patients to rapid supraventricular rhythms and organized atrial flutter circuits. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as the presence of supraventricular tachycardia or atrial flutter may signal underlying congestive cardiomyopathy and guide diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at both the structural heart disease and its rhythm complications.

Overall, the interconnected concepts highlight that tachyarrhythmias such as تسرع القلب الانتيابي, various forms of heart block (إِحْصار القلب غير محدد), and other conduction disorders share common anatomic substrates (the AV node), overlapping clinical presentations, and related therapeutic strategies, including the use of adenosine for tachycardia, avoidance of adenosine in heart block, and implantation of pacemakers for persistent conduction failure.
